claim.thesis: Surface the years and documents where nationalist discipline becomes a strong semantic organizing force.

never once materialised, and the practical eye looks in

vain to-day for any marked signs of prosperity due to

the propaganda. True, we may have two gombeen

men where only one existed before, but who will say

this is progress. In what way can it be associated

with the current .catch-cry *‘ better farming, better

living,”” and so on?  The farce is too thin to pass

muster at this hour of day. Not many years ago we

heard and read much of poultry fattening in Ireland.

-There was not a chicken or old hen to get a pass out of

i the country without first being stuffed to bursting

| point.  All that was required was money from the

Department. Well, the Department supplied the

money and subsidised basket-snappers who competed

in the markets with the older order, and shipped the

A VIGILANCE CONGRESS—WHY AND WHEN?

REAT things have been accomplished in a short

¥ time by the Vigilance Committees in seriously

crippling the movements of the reptile Press. But, we

have scotched the snake, not killed it. Well organised,

wealthy, commercial companies are in the background,

waiting for the fervour of moral zealots to fizzle out,

and ready, at the first sign of weakness, to spread their

poison abroad, and to spare no expense in attempting

to do so. ! 5

The weak point in the campaign, as several actively

interested have felt, is, that while we have many parti-

cular and local organisations, we have no central execu-

tive linking these together and giving thera a united

contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
sioner Yeats: indeed we enjoy hearing raucous cries of

““ bigot”’ shouted at us. We at least have the measure

of the Sourfaces, and are not afraid to publish the

fact.

The real fact is that Irish Catholics are a minority

of the working entity—Great Britain and Ireland. The

Treemasons in Ireland and such like have the ear of

Greab Britain. From Dan, Dan, Danny MecCann on-

wards they are playing into that ear. So vigorously

are they playing that we cannot even recount the re-

cent tunes. There was some alleged case in Cork, and

some fellow named Kingston or Kingstown was ex-

ported to do the gramaphone over in England. But

whatever little thing, real or alleged, happens that

may be represented against Irish Catholics, the majo-

g '.l'hg actual work of the Association will consist in

qualifying soldiers for settlement or employment on

the land either at home, or, when their liability to re-

call as Reservists has terminated, in the oversea Em-

pire, and afterwards in finding openings for them. It

is intended, as the first step, to establish training farms

at home where the men, preferably during the period

of service in the Reserve, will acquire knowledge of

agriculture and erafts which will be useful to them as

colonists, farmers or employés. The practical applica-

smmense  demonstrations were written by him for the *‘Cork
Examiner,”’ and afterwards copied into the Dublin and English press.

0'Neill Daunt in his ““Ireland and her Agitators,” the last edition of

)

which appeared in 1888 as ‘85 years of Irish History,” pays a fine
sribute to Lenihan for his services as a political organiser and journalist.
The ‘‘Liberator”’ warmly admired him, and when. Lenihan founded the
« Tipperary Vindicator” at Nenagh in 1844, O’Connell wrote him a
very appreciative Tetter and for half a century this letter was on view
in his office in Limerick, to which city he removed in 1849 on taking

1)

over the ‘‘ Limerick Reporter,” a journal he had previously edited for
two years, 1841-43, when he accepted an engagement from John Francis
Maguire on the staff of tho ‘‘ Cork Examiner.”’ It was during Lenihan’s
editorship of the  Tipperary Vindicator ”’ that he exposed the famous
police plot known as ‘‘ The Shinrone Conspiracy,” and obtained the
dismissal of a large number of constables with their leader.

To ono of his marked hiterary gifts and antiquarian ta,q‘tes Limerick
